---
title: "Functional Data Modeling in R for Clustering and Regression"
author: "Julien JACQUES"
date: "Université Lumière Lyon 2, FRANCE"
output: beamer_presentation
toc: false
slide_level: 3
---

# Some applications with sequential data

### Growth curves
\small
Heights in centimeters of 54 girls at 31 ages:
```{r, out.width='60%',message=FALSE,warning=FALSE}
library(fda)
data("growth")
with(growth, matplot(age, hgtf[, 1:10], type="b"))
```

- *J.O. Ramsay, B.W. Silverman (2010). Functional Data Analysis. Springer.*

### Temperature curves
\small
Daily temperature at different locations in Canada, averaged over 1960 to 1994.
```{r, out.width='60%',message=FALSE,warning=FALSE}
data(CanadianWeather)
matplot(day.5,CanadianWeather$dailyAv[,,"Temperature.C"],
      type="l", axes=FALSE, xlab="",ylab="")
```

- *J.O. Ramsay, B.W. Silverman (2010). Functional Data Analysis. Springer.*


### Collaboration with CWD iJump
Accelerometer and gyroscope measurement on a horse saddle
![](images/iJump-1.png){width=160px}
![](images/iJump-data.png){width=240px}

### Collaboration with CWD iJump
Goal: **to predict horse speed and stride length from accelerometer and gyroscope data**

![](images/iJump-2-1.png){width=180px}
![](images/iJump-2-2.png){width=180px}

- [video FR3 Aquitaine](https://lim-group.com/wp-content/videos/FR3_Aquitaine_iJump.mp4?_=1)

\small 
- *A. Schmutz, J. Jacques, C. Bouveyron, L. Chèze and P. Martin). Clustering multivariate functional data in group-specific functional subspaces, Computational Statistics, 35, 1101-1131, 2020.*\
- *A. Schmutz, J. Jacques, L. Chèze and P. Martin. A method to estimate horse speed per stride from one IMU with a machine learning method. Sensors, 2020, 20 (2), 12 p, 2020.*

### Collaboration with Arpege Master K
Arpege MasterK is a major company in industrial weighing which develops weighing solutions based on digital sensors

::: columns
:::: {.column width=30%}
![](images/MasterK-0.png){width=130px}
::::
:::: {.column width=30%}
![](images/MasterK-01.jpg){width=130px}
::::
:::: {.column width=30%}
![](images/MasterK-1.pdf){width=110px}
::::
:::

During their activity, these sensors produce data from which the measurement activity is carried out.


### Collaboration with Arpege Master K
- data varies greatly from one measurement to another...

![](images/Courbes_gros_volume_line.pdf){width=400px}

- sometimes sensor failures produce atypical data that should be **detected**... and **anticipated**

Goal: **to perform clustering and outliers detection**

\small
- *M. Amovin, I. Gannaz, J. Jacques (2022). Outlier detection in multivariate functional data through a contaminated mixture model. Computational Statistics and Data Analysis, 174, 107496.*
- *Patent n°4386334. Dynamic weighing system based on shaft group weighing.*


### Collaboration with EDF
Thanks to Linky meter, EDF can precisely measure consumption curve for every customers.
![](images/Linky-1.jpg){width=200px}![](images/Linky-data.pdf){width=240px}

For marketing objective, EDF want to better understand Linky data, and for this produce summary

Goal: **to perform co-clustering (customers / days)**

### Collaboration with EDF

![](images/Linky-res-1.pdf){width=105px}
![](images/Linky-res-2.pdf){width=125px}
![](images/Linky-res-3.pdf){width=155px}

\small 
*C. Bouveyron, L. Bozzi L., J. Jacques J. and F-X. Jollois. The Functional Latent Block Model for the Co-Clustering of Electricity Consumption Curves, Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series C, 67 [4], 897-915, 2018.*


### Collaboration with Orange
Network operator as Orange should manage the quality of the network.

![QoE for video streeming](images/mos_curves.pdf){width=200px}
![KPI for video streeming](images/mssim_curves.pdf){width=200px}

Goal: **to predict the Quality of Experience** (MOS) of video streaming **according to some KPI** (MS-SSIM)\


### Cycling data
::: columns
:::: {.column width=20%}
![](images/Garmin.jpeg){width=100px}
::::
:::: {.column width=80%}
```{r, echo=F, out.height='60%'}
load('cycling9.Rdata')
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
i=24
library(ggplot2)
plot(1:60,cycling9$WATTS[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='Power (Watts)',type='l',col=2)
plot(1:60,cycling9$HR[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=3,ylim=c(0,200))
lines(1:60,cycling9$CAD[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=4)
lines(1:60,cycling9$KPH[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=5)
lines(1:60,cycling9$SLOPE[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=6)
lines(1:60,cycling9$TEMP[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=7)
lines(1:60,cycling9$ALT[i,1201:1260],xlab='Time (seconds)',ylab='',type='l',col=8)
legend('topleft', legend=c("Hearth Rate (BPM)", "Cadence (RPM)", "Speed (KPH)","Slope","Temperature","Altitude"),col=3:8, lty=1, cex=1)
```
::::
:::
\small 

Goal: **To explain the power I develop during my cycling session according to my hearth rate, my pedalling cadence, my speed, the slope of the road, the outdoor temperature and the altitude**

*data available at https://github.com/JuJacques/Functional-Data*

# Introduction to Functional Data

### Temporal data or sequential data
In all the previous applications, we have to work with a sequence $x_i(t_1),\ldots,x_i(t_m)$ of **temporal data** or **sequential data**, where:

- the number of sample points $m$ is generally **large**
- the sampling times $t_1,\ldots,t_m$:
    - are sometimes **irregular**
    - **not** necessarily **identical** for each observation $i$
    
```{r, out.width='50%', echo=F, fig.align = "center"}
x1=c(1,2,4,5,6,8,9)
x2=c(1,2,3,5,6,9,11,13,14,15)
plot(x1,rnorm(length(x1)),type='b',xlab="time",ylab="",ylim=c(-3,3),xlim=c(0,16),xaxt="n", yaxt="n",lwd=2)
lines(x2,rnorm(length(x2)),col=2,type='b',lwd=2)
legend('topleft',legend=c("ind. 1","ind. 2"),text.col=1:2,lty = 1,lwd=2,col = 1:2)
abline(v=x1,lty=2,col=1,lwd=.2)
abline(v=x2,lty=2,col=2,lwd=.2)
```

**Problem**: in most statistical learning applications (clustering, classification, regression, forecasting...), we **should observe exactly the same features for each individual**.

### Temporal data or sequential data
Working with such sequential data is also difficult:

- because of the strong **correlation** between observations at close time steps (which produce large variance of estimators)
- when the number of time steps is large: **curse of dimensionality**

### Functional data approach
Functional data approach assumes that $x_i(t_1),\ldots,x_i(t_m)$ are **discrete observations of a function**: $x_i(t) \quad \mbox{with }t\in [0,T]$
```{r, out.width='70%', warning=F, message=F, echo=F, fig.align = "center"}
library(fda)
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
plot(growth$age,growth$hgtm[,1],xlab='age',ylab='height',cex.lab=1.5,cex.axis=1.5,cex.main=1.5,main='discrete observations')
plot(growth$age,growth$hgtm[,1],xlab='age',ylab='height',cex.lab=1.5,cex.axis=1.5,type='l',cex.main=1.5,main='latent function x(t)')
```

So, rather than describing each individual in specific high-dimensional multivariate space, we consider all of them in a single functional space.

### Functional random variable and functional data

- A **functional variable** $X$ is a random variable which takes values into an infinite dimensional space (*functional space*):
$$X=\{X(t),t\in T\}$$
- if $T$ is some interval of time: $X$ is a continuous-time process
- A **functional data** is its observation $x=\{x(t),t\in T\}$:
  - $T\in\mathbb{R}$ (time, wavelength...): random curve
  - $T\in\mathbb{R}^2$ (space)...: random surface
- A **functional data set** is the observation of $n$ realizations $x_1,\ldots,x_n$ of $X$.


### Functional data bibliography
![](images/FDA.jpg){width=120px}
![](images/FDA-R.pdf){width=120px}
![](images/NPFDA.jpg){width=120px}

### Functional vs multivariate approach
Advantages of the Functional Data approach:

- allow to **parsimoniously** model the **intrinsic regularity** of the curves (and avoid to work with highly correlated features)
- allows to get rid of **irregularities** in the sampling step
- allows to take into account any **observation noise**
- allows to work with the **derivatives** of the functions (which can carry additional information)

### Specificities of the Functional Data approach
From a practical point of view, working in a functional space is not so obvious:

- distances are integrals, which should *generally* be approximated: 
$${||x_i(t)-x_j(t)||}^2=\int_{[0,T]}(x_i(t)-x_j(t))^2dt$$
- the notion of probability density function is generally not well defined$^\dagger$
- the curves $x_i(t)$ are never totally observed: we only have access to a finite set of discrete observations $x_i(t_1),\ldots,x_i(t_m)$

\vfill
\small
$^\dagger$ *A. Delaigle and P. Hall. Defining probability density for a distribution of random functions. Ann. Statist., 38 (2), 1171-1193, 2010.* 


### Summary
\tableofcontents

# From raw data to functional data

### Functional data in practice
If working with the functions $x_i (t)$ rather than with their discrete observations has a lot of advantages, we must not forget that **in practice, we never have access to these functions** $x_i (t)$ but only to discrete observations of these $x_i(t_1),\ldots,x_i(t_m)$.

A first step in functional data approach is often to **reconstruct the functional character** of the data, most often using smoothing techniques.

### Functional reconstruction
One simple approach is to suppose that the curve $x(t)$ can be decomposed into a base of functions $(\phi_j(t))_{1\leq j\leq p}$ of finite dimension $p$:
$$x(t)=\sum_{j=1}^pc_j\phi_j(t)$$
and that we observe at some $t_i$:
\begin{eqnarray*}
y_i&=&x(t_i)+\epsilon_i\\
&=&\sum_{j=1}^pc_j\phi_j(t_i)+\epsilon_i
\end{eqnarray*}

Several choices of bases are possible: Fourier, spline, wavelets, ... depending on the nature and regularity of the data

### Fourier basis
\small
When some periodic pattern occur in the curves:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\phi_0(t)&\propto&1\\
\phi_{2r-1}(t)&\propto&\sin(r\omega t)\\
\phi_{2r}(t)&\propto&\cos(r\omega t)\\
\end{eqnarray*}
where $\omega$ defines the period $2\pi/\omega$
```{r, fig.height = 3, fig.width = 4, fig.align = "center"}
plot(create.fourier.basis(c(0,1),nbasis=5,period=1/2))
```

### B-spline basis
\small
For regular and non periodic curves:

- interval $[0,T]$ split into sub-intervals
- polynomial of order $m$ on each sub-interval
- continuity constraints of polynomials (and their derivatives)

```{r, fig.height = 2, fig.width = 5, fig.align = "center"}
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(create.bspline.basis(norder=1,breaks = c(0,0.5,1)))
plot(create.bspline.basis(norder=2,breaks = c(0,0.5,1)))
plot(create.bspline.basis(norder=3,breaks = c(0,0.5,1)))
```

### Reconstruction of the functional nature of data with R
\small
We select one curve:
```{r, out.width='70%', warning=F, message=F}
x=growth$hgtf[,1]
plot(growth$age,x,xlab='age',ylab='height',
        cex.lab=2,cex.axis=2)
```

### Reconstruction of the functional nature of data with R
\small
1. Defining the basis of function
```{r}
base <- create.bspline.basis(range(growth$age), nbasis=5, 
                             norder=4)
```

2. Smoothing the data on the basis 
```{r, out.width='70%', warning=F, message=F}
xlisse <- smooth.basis(growth$age, x, base)
str(xlisse)
```

### Reconstruction of the functional nature of data with R
\small
3. Plot the result
```{r, out.width='70%', warning=F, message=F}
plot(growth$age,x,xlab='age',ylab='height')
lines(xlisse,col=2)
```

### Influence of the basis choice
\small
```{r, out.width='60%', warning=F, message=F}
base1<-create.bspline.basis(range(growth$age),nbasis=5,norder=1)
base2<-create.bspline.basis(range(growth$age),nbasis=5,norder=2)
base3<-create.bspline.basis(range(growth$age),nbasis=5,norder=3)
plot(growth$age,x,xlab='age',ylab='height')
lines(smooth.basis(growth$age, x, base1),col=2)
lines(smooth.basis(growth$age, x, base2),col=3)
lines(smooth.basis(growth$age, x, base3),col=4)
```

### Choice of the basis and its size
- in supervised learning (classification, regression): 
  - can be optimized according to the predictive objective
- in unsupervised learning (clustering, PCA): 
  - empirically by visually observing the reconstruction 
  - by cross-validation on the functional data reconstruction

### Computation of a derivative
\small
Sometime, the derivative of the function carry out important information
```{r, out.width='90%', fig.height = 3, fig.width = 6, fig.align = "center", message=F,cache=TRUE}
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
p=plot(xlisse,xlab='age',ylab='height')
p=plot(deriv.fd(xlisse$fd))
p=plot(deriv.fd(xlisse$fd,2))
```

### Lab 1
\small
- Plot bspline smoothing for the Canadian daily temperatures
```{r, out.width='50%',message=FALSE,warning=FALSE,size="footnotesize"}
library(fda)
data(CanadianWeather)
basis <- create.bspline.basis(c(0, 365),nbasis=10,norder=3)
daytempfd <- smooth.basis(day.5, 
        CanadianWeather$dailyAv[,,"Temperature.C"],
        basis, fdnames=list("Day", "Station", "Deg C"))$fd
invisible(plot(daytempfd))
```

- Try to use different basis


### Summary
\tableofcontents


# Functional principal components analysis


### Functional Principal Components Analysis (FPCA)

For standard multivariate data, PCA allows visualization in low dimensionality (2) of data initially in high dimensionality:

- the low-dimensional space is defined by factorial axes
- these factorial axes are a linear combination of the initial variables
- they summarize the main sources of variation in the data

In FPCA:

- the low-dimensional space space is defined by *factorial functions*
- these factorial functions summarize the main sources of variation in the data

### Covariance operator
\small
The functional covariance is defined by:
$$\mathcal{C}(x(s),x(t))=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i(s)-\bar{x}(s))(x_i(t)-\bar{x}(t))$$
```{r,  fig.height = 3, fig.width = 4, fig.align = "center"}
filled.contour(day.5,day.5,
               cor.fd(day.5,daytempfd), key.border=NA)
```

### Karhunen-Loève decomposition
Karhunen-Loève decomposition of $X$
$$X(t) = \mu(t)+ \sum_{j\geq 1}Z_{j}f_{j}(t),$$

- where $f_{j}$ form an othronormal basis, and are eigen functions of the covariance operator:
$$\mathcal{C} {f_{j}}=\lambda_{j}f_{j}$$
- $Z_{j}$ are uncorrelated centred random variables (principal component) of variance $\lambda_{j}$, $\lambda_1\geq \lambda_2\geq\ldots$
$$Z_{j} = \int_0^T \langle {X}(t)-\mu(t),f_j(t)\rangle dt$$

### FPCA in practice
- in practice $\mathcal{C}$ is unknown
- when the functions $x_{i}$ are decomposed into a basis of functions $\{\phi_{j}(t)\}_j$, the functional eigen system becomes a matricial eigen system: $Z_{j}$ and $\lambda_j$ are then solutions of
$$\frac{1}{n-1}C W C' Z_j = \lambda_j Z_j,$$
where
  - $C$ is the centred matrix of basis expansion coefficients $c_{ij}$ 
  - $W$ is the matrix of inner products between basis functions $w_{k\ell}=\int_0^T f_k(t)f_\ell(t) dt$
 
### FPCA with R
\small
```{r}
fpca <- pca.fd(daytempfd, nharm=4)
```

Percentage of explained variance by the **functional principal components**
```{r, out.width='70%'}
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
plot(fpca$varprop,type="b")
plot(fpca$harmonics)
```

### FPCA with R
Plot of $\bar{x}(t)\pm2 \sqrt{\lambda_k}f_k(t)$ can help interpretation of the principal functions:
```{r, out.width='80%'}
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot.pca.fd(fpca, cex.main=0.9)
```

### FPCA with R
\small
Finally we can plot of the observations
```{r, out.width='70%'}
plot(fpca$scores[,1:2],col=0)
region=as.numeric(as.factor(CanadianWeather$region))
print(levels(as.factor(CanadianWeather$region)))
text(fpca$scores[,1:2],labels=colnames(daytempfd$coefs),
     col=region)
```

### Lab 2
\small
Perform FPCA for the precipitation curves
```{r, out.width='60%',message=FALSE,warning=FALSE,size="footnotesize"}
dayprecipfd <- smooth.basis(day.5, 
        CanadianWeather$dailyAv[,,"Precipitation.mm"],
        basis, fdnames=list("Day", "Station", "Deg C"))$fd
fpca <- pca.fd(dayprecipfd, nharm=4)
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot.pca.fd(fpca, cex.main=0.9)
```

### Lab 2
\small
```{r, out.width='60%',message=FALSE,warning=FALSE,size="footnotesize"}
plot(fpca$scores[,1:2],col=0)
region=as.numeric(as.factor(CanadianWeather$region))
print(levels(as.factor(CanadianWeather$region)))
text(fpca$scores[,1:2],labels=colnames(daytempfd$coefs),
     col=region)
```


# Clustering of functional data


### Clustering

Clustering  is the task of **grouping a set of objects** in such a way that objects in the same group (**cluster**) are more similar to each other than to those in other groups 

### Geometric versus probabilistic clustering
- If historical clustering methods are **geometric** (kmeans, hierchical clustering), based on the notion of distance
- modern clustering methods are **probabilistic**:
  - a cluster is defined as a set of observation arising from a same (simple, unimodal) distribution
    $$x|z=k\sim f_k(x,\theta_k)$$
    where $z$ indicates the cluster membership
  - marginal distribution of the observation is a (parametric) **mixture**:
  $$x\sim \sum_k\pi_kf_k(x,\theta_k)$$

### Advantages of probabilistic clustering

Formal mathematical definition of probabilistic clustering allows to use model selection criterion (BIC, ICL) in order to:

- choose the most appropriated distribution
- choose the number of clusters

BIC aims to select the model $m$ maximizing the (log) posterior probability
$$-2\log p(m|x)\approx BIC(m)=-2\log p_m(x|\hat{\theta}_m)+K_m\log n$$
where $K_m$ is the dimension of $\theta_m$

### Functional data clustering
Functional data clustering method can be classified into 3 categories$^{\dag}$:

- geometric method, based on semi-metric for functional data:
$$d_p(x_i(t),x_j(t))=\left(\int_{[0,T]}(x_i^{(p)}(t)-x_j^{(p)}(t))^2dt\right)^{1/2}$$
where $x_i^{(p)}(t)$ is the p-th derivative of $x_i(t)$

- two-step methods which plug the functional data into a finite-dimensional setting before using usual clustering method 
- model-based methods which relies on a probabilistic modeling of the functional data

\bigskip
\small 
*$^{\dag}$ J. Jacques and C. Preda (2014), Functional data clustering: a survey, Advances in Data Analysis and Classification, 8[3], 231-255.*

### Model-based functional clustering
The **notion of probability density** for functional random variables generally **does not exist**$^{\dag}$

Delaigle \& Hall (2010) shown that the probability of $X\in L_{2}([0,T])$ to belong to a ball of radius $h$ centred in $x\in L_2[0,T]$ can be approximated using the probability density of the functional principal component scores (Karhunen-Loeve expansion coefficients)

Several works define model-based clustering algorithms based on:

- Gaussian mixtures for **functional principal component scores**,
- which are equivalent to Gaussian mixtures for **basis expansion coefficients**

\bigskip
\small 
*$^{\dag}$A. Delaigle and P. Hall. Defining probability density for a distribution of random functions. The Annals of Statistics, 38:1171–1193, 2010.*

### Model-based functional clustering
According to Delaigle \& Hall (2010), the notion of density prob. of ${X}(t)$ can be approximated by the p.d.f. of the FPCA scores $Z_j$:
\begin{equation*}
{X}(t)=\mu(t)+\sum_{j\geq 1}Z_j{f}_j(t).
\end{equation*}
<!-- where ${f}_j(t)$ are the eigen-functions solutions of  -->
<!-- \begin{equation*} -->
<!-- \mathcal{V}{f}_j=\lambda_j{f}_j -->
<!-- \end{equation*} -->
<!-- with $\mathcal{V}$ the covariance operator of ${X}(t)$ defined by: -->
<!-- \begin{equation*} -->
<!-- \mathcal{V}{f}=\int_{0}^TV(\cdot,t){f}(t)dt -->
<!-- \end{equation*} -->
<!-- whose kernel $V$ is: -->
<!-- \begin{equation*} -->
<!-- V(s,t)=E[ ({X}(t)-\mu(t)) \otimes ({X}(s)-\mu(s))] -->
<!-- \end{equation*} -->

### Model-based clustering for multivariate functional data
Schmutz et a. (2020)$^{\dag}$ proposes a model-based clustering algorithm (**funHDDC**) for multivariate functional data
$$\textbf{x}_1(t),\ldots,\textbf{x}_n(t)$$
where each observation is a set of $p$ curves $$\textbf{x}_i(t)=(x_i^1(t),\ldots,x_i^p(t))$$

\bigskip
\small 
*$^{\dag}$A. Schmutz, J. Jacques, C. Bouveyron, L. Chèze and P. Martin (2020). Clustering multivariate functional data in group-specific functional subspaces, Computational Statistics, 35, 1101-1131.*

*Corresponding R package: funHDDC*

### funHDDC - Multivariate Functional Data
Basis expansion assumptions for multivariate FD: 
\begin{equation*}
\boldsymbol{X}(t)=\boldsymbol{C}\boldsymbol{\phi}'(t).
\end{equation*}
\small
with\
$\textbf{C}=
\begin{pmatrix}
	c_{11}^{1} & ... & c_{1R_{1}}^{1}  & c_{11}^{2}  &  ...  & c_{1R_{2}}^{2} & ... & c_{11}^{p} & ... & c_{1R_{p}}^{p}\\
	& & & & ... \\
	c_{n1}^{1} & ... & c_{nR_{1}}^{1}  & c_{n1}^{2}  &  ...  & c_{nR_{2}}^{2} & ... & c_{n1}^{p} & ... & c_{nR_{p}}^{p}
\end{pmatrix}$\
and\
$\boldsymbol{\phi(t)}=
\begin{pmatrix}
	\phi_{1}^{1}(t) & ... & \phi^{1}_{R_{1}}(t)  &      0  &  ...  & 0 & ...& 0 & ... & 0\\
	      0    &     ...     &  0    &      \phi^{2}_{1}(t) &  ... & \phi^{2}_{R_{2}}(t)  & ...&   0   &      ...   &     0 \\
				& & & & ... \\
				0  &       ...       &  0  & 0 & ... & 0 & ...&     \phi^{p}_{1}(t) & ... &  \phi^{p}_{R_{p}}(t) 
\end{pmatrix}$

### funHDDC - Multivariate Functional PCA (MFPCA)
Karhunen-Loève expansion hold in the multivariate case:
\begin{equation*}
\boldsymbol{X}(t)=\mu(t)+\sum_{j\geq 1}Z_j\boldsymbol{f}_j(t).
\end{equation*}
in which the eigen-function are multivariate. 

Under the basis expansion assumption, the eigen-problem
\begin{equation*}
\mathcal{V}\boldsymbol{f}_j=\lambda_j\boldsymbol{f}_j
\end{equation*}
becomes a classical matrix eigen-problem:
\begin{equation*}
\frac{1}{n-1}\tilde{\boldsymbol{C}}\boldsymbol{W}\tilde{\boldsymbol{C}}'b_j=\lambda_jb_j
\end{equation*}
where 

- $\tilde{\boldsymbol{C}}$ is the centered version of $\boldsymbol{C}$: $\tilde{\boldsymbol{C}}=(I_n-\frac{1}{n}1_n'1_n){\boldsymbol{C}}$
- $\boldsymbol{W}=\int_0^T\boldsymbol{\phi(t)}'\boldsymbol{\phi(t)}dt$ is the matrix of inner product between basis function

### funHDDC - Multivariate Functional PCA (MFPCA)
The MFPCA scores are obtained by:
$$Z_{j} = \boldsymbol{C}\boldsymbol{W}b'_j \in\mathbb{R}^n$$
Consequently, each multivariate curve $\boldsymbol{x}_i$ can be identified by its finite-dimensional MFPCA scores $Z_i=(Z_{ij})_{1\leq j \leq \sum_jR_j}$


### funHDDC - Probability distribution assumption
The Multivariate FPCA scores $Z_i$ are assumed to follow a mixture of Gaussian
\begin{equation*}
Z_i \sim \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(m_k,\Delta_k)
\end{equation*}
with the following parsimony assumption:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\small{\boldsymbol{\Delta}_{k}=\left(\begin{array}{c@{}c}
\begin{array}{|ccc|}
\hline a_{k1} &  & 0\\
 & \ddots & \\
0 &  & a_{kd_{k}}\\\hline \end{array} & \mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0} & \begin{array}{|cccc|}
\hline b_{k} &  &  & 0\\
 & \ddots &  & \\
 &  & \ddots & \\
0 &  &  & b_{k}\\\hline \end{array}\end{array}\right)\begin{array}{cc}
\left.\begin{array}{c}
\,\\
\,\\
\,\end{array}\right\}  & d_{k}\vspace{1.5ex}\\
\left.\begin{array}{c}
\,\\
\,\\
\,\\
\,\\
\,\end{array}\right\}  & R-d_{k}\end{array}\label{eqdelta}}
\end{eqnarray*}

### funHDDC - Probability distribution assumption
Assuming that the MFPCA scores are Gaussian is equivalent to assume that the basis expansion coefficient $C_i$ ($i$-th row of $\boldsymbol{C}$) are Gaussian:
\begin{equation*}
C_i \sim \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mu_k,\Sigma_k)
\end{equation*}
with :

- $\mu_k=\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}U_km_k$
- $\Sigma_k=\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}U_k\Delta_kU_k'\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}+\Xi_k$

### funHDDC - Parameter estimation
\small
The observed log-likelihood:
\begin{equation*}
\ell(\theta,\boldsymbol{c})=\sum_{i=1}^n\log\left(\sum_{k=1}^K\pi_k\psi(c_i,\underbrace{\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}U_km_k}_{\mu_k},\underbrace{\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}U_k\Delta_kU_k'\boldsymbol{W}^{-1/2}+\Xi_k}_{\Sigma_k})\right)
\end{equation*}
where $\theta=(\pi_k,\mu_k,a_{k1},\ldots,a_{kd_k},b_k,d_k,U_k)_{1 \leq k\leq K}$ and $\psi$ the Gaussian pdf, **is not maximizable in a closed form**

### funHDDC - Parameter estimation
Let introduce $Z_i=(Z_{i1},\ldots,Z_{iK})$ with $Z_{ik}=1$ if the multivariate curve $i$ belong to clsuter $k$, 0 otherwise.

The complete log-likelihood is more easy to maximize, as long as we have the cluster membership $\boldsymbol{z}$
\begin{equation*}
\ell(\theta,\boldsymbol{c},\boldsymbol{z})=\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{k=1}^Kz_{ik}\log\left(\pi_k\psi(c_i,\mu_k,\Sigma_k)\right)
\end{equation*}


### funHDDC - EM algorithm
We use the EM algorithm, which start with an initialization $\theta^{(0)}$ of $\theta$, and iterates until convergence of the log-likelihood:

- E step: compute
\begin{equation*}
E[\ell(\theta,\boldsymbol{c},\boldsymbol{z})|\boldsymbol{c},\theta^{(q)}]
\end{equation*}
which involves to compute $E[z_{ik}|\boldsymbol{c},\theta^{(q)}]$
- M step:
\begin{equation*}
\theta^{(q+1)}=\underset{\theta}{\operatorname{argmax}} E[\ell(\theta,\boldsymbol{c},\boldsymbol{z})|\boldsymbol{c},\theta^{(q)}]
\end{equation*}

### funHDDC - Clusters number selection
On advantage of working with model-based approach of clustering, is the possibility to use model-section criterion to select the number of clusters:
\begin{equation*}
BIC_k = -2\ell(\hat{\theta},\boldsymbol{c})+\nu \log(n)
\end{equation*}
where $\nu$ is the number of model parameters.

BIC can also be used to select:

- the hyper-parameter $d_k$
- the most appropriate one among different parsimony assumption

### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\small
Perform a clustering of the Canadian temperature curves
```{r,message=FALSE,warning=FALSE,size="scriptsize"}
library(funHDDC)
res<-funHDDC(daytempfd,K=2,model="AkjBkQkDk",
             init="random",threshold=0.2)
```
Try different sub-models and different number of clusters

### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\small
Plot the mean curves of the 2 clusters.
```{r, out.width='70%'}
s1<-fd(daytempfd$coefs[,which(res$class==1)],daytempfd$basis)
s2<-fd(daytempfd$coefs[,which(res$class==2)],daytempfd$basis)
invisible(plot(mean.fd(s1),col=1,ylim=c(-20,20),lty=1,lwd=3))
lines(mean.fd(s2),col=2,lty=1,lwd=3)
```


### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\small
Map of the results
```{r, out.width='50%'}
library(maps)
map("world", "canada")
text(-CanadianWeather$coordinates[,2],
     CanadianWeather$coordinates[,1],
     labels=rownames(CanadianWeather$coordinates),
     col=res$class,cex=0.75)
```

### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\small
With 3 clusters
```{r, message=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
set.seed(4)
res3<-funHDDC(daytempfd,K=3,model="AkjBkQkDk",
             init="random",threshold=0.2)
```

Note that BIC is better than for 2 clusters (*the implemented BIC should be maximized*)

### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\small
With 3 clusters
```{r, message=TRUE, warning=FALSE, out.width='50%'}
map("world", "canada")
text(-CanadianWeather$coordinates[,2],
     CanadianWeather$coordinates[,1],
     labels=rownames(CanadianWeather$coordinates),
     col=res3$class,cex=0.75)
```

### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\scriptsize
We can now cluster multivariate functional data, i.e. temperature and precipitation
```{r, message=TRUE, warning=FALSE, out.width='50%'}
daybasis65<-create.fourier.basis(c(0, 365), nbasis=65, period=365)
daytempfd<-smooth.basis(day.5, CanadianWeather$dailyAv[,,"Temperature.C"],
                daybasis65,fdnames=list("Day", "Station", "Deg C"))$fd
dayprecfd<-smooth.basis(day.5, CanadianWeather$dailyAv[,,"Precipitation.mm"], 
                daybasis65, fdnames=list("Day", "Station", "Mm"))$fd
res.multi<-funHDDC(list(daytempfd,dayprecfd),K=3,model="AkjBkQkDk",
                init="random",threshold=0.2)
```


### Lab 3: functional data clustering
\scriptsize
Graphical representation of cluster means
```{r, message=TRUE, warning=FALSE, out.width='50%'}
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
select1<-fd(daytempfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==1)],daytempfd$basis)
select2<-fd(daytempfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==2)],daytempfd$basis)
select3<-fd(daytempfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==3)],daytempfd$basis)
invisible(plot(mean.fd(select1),ylim=c(-20,20),lty=1,lwd=3))
lines(mean.fd(select2),col=2,lty=1,lwd=3)
lines(mean.fd(select3),col=3,lty=1,lwd=3)
select1b<-fd(dayprecfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==1)],dayprecfd$basis)
select2b<-fd(dayprecfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==2)],dayprecfd$basis)
select3b<-fd(dayprecfd$coefs[,which(res.multi$class==3)],dayprecfd$basis)
invisible(plot(mean.fd(select1b),ylim=c(0,5),lty=1,lwd=3))
lines(mean.fd(select2b),col=2,lty=1,lwd=3)
lines(mean.fd(select3b),col=3,lty=1,lwd=3)
```

# Regression with functional data


### Regression with functional data
The task is now regression with functional data:
$$Y = \beta X + \epsilon,$$
with typ. $Y,X\in L^2[0,T]$.

Depending on the nature of $Y$ and $X$, we talk about:

| $Y$      | $X$ | Regression type      |
|----------|--------------|----------------------|
| scalar   | function     | scalar-on-function   |
| function | scalar       | function-on-scalar   |
| function | function     | function-on-function |

### Application for function-on-function regression
To explain the daily **precipitation** according to **temperature**, in Canadian weather stations

```{r, echo=F, out.height='70%'}
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
library(fda)
matplot(day.5, CanadianWeather$dailyAv[, , "Precipitation.mm"],
        type="l", axes=FALSE, xlab="", ylab="Precipitation (mm)",main="Y: Precipitation")
axis(2, las=1)
axis(1, monthBegin.5, labels=FALSE)
axis(1, monthEnd.5, labels=FALSE)
axis(1, monthMid, monthLetters, tick=FALSE)

matplot(day.5, CanadianWeather$dailyAv[, , "Temperature.C"],
        type="l", axes=FALSE, xlab="", ylab="Mean Temperature (deg C)",main="X: Temperature")
axis(2, las=1)
axis(1, monthBegin.5, labels=FALSE)
axis(1, monthEnd.5, labels=FALSE)
axis(1, monthMid, monthLetters, tick=FALSE)
```

### The concurrent model for function-on-function regression
In order to modelize $y_i(t)$ in function of the $x_i^\ell(t)$, we can consider the **concurrent model**$^\dagger$: 
\begin{eqnarray*}
    {y}_i(t) &=& \beta_0(t) + \sum_{\ell=1}^p\beta_\ell(t){x}^\ell_i(t) + \varepsilon_i(t)\\
    &=& \mathbf{x}_i(t)\beta(t)+ \varepsilon_i(t)
\end{eqnarray*}
where the residual $\varepsilon_i(t)\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$.

This model assumes that the value of $Y(t)$ at time $t$ depend directly on the value of the covariate ${x}^\ell(t)$ at the **same time**, through functional coefficients $\beta_\ell(t)$.

\vfill
\small
$^\dagger$ *Ramsay, J., Silverman, B.W.: Functional Data Analysis, 2nd edn. Springer Series
in Statistics. Springer, New York (2005).*

### function-on-function regression in R
\small
Several works and R package implements concurrent model estimation

- Esitmation through a penalized mixed model:

Ivanescu, A., Staicu, A.-M., Scheipl, F., Greven, S.: Penalized function-on-
function regression. Computational Statistics 30(2), 539–568 (2015)
```{r}
library(refund)
```

- Wavelet transformation approach:

Luo, R., Qi, X., Wang, Y.: Functional wavelet regression for linear function-on-
function models. Electronic Journal of Statistics 10(2), 3179–3216 (2016) 
```{r, message=FALSE}
library(FRegSigCom) 
```

### function-on-function regression in R
\small
- Basis decomposition with regularization and forecasting intervals :

Jean Steve Tamo Tchomgui, Julien Jacques, Vincent Barriac, Guillaume Fraysse, Stéphane Chrétien. A Penalized Spline Estimator for Functional Linear Regression with Functional Response. Advances in Data Analysis and Classification, 2026.

https://github.com/Orange-OpenSource/penffr

### Heterogeneous data set
- In some applications, data sets are heterogeneous and a single regression model is not sufficient

- Solutions: to consider a **mixture of concurrent models**$^\dagger$

\vfill
\small
$^\dagger$ *J.S. Tamo Tchomgui, J. Jacques, V. Barriac, G. Fraysse, S. Chrétien: A mixture of experts regression model for functional response with functional covariates. Statistics and Computing, 34[124] (2024).*

R code: https://github.com/Orange-OpenSource/penffr

### Function-on-Function Mixture of Expert (FFMoE)
Let $Z\in\{1,\ldots,K\}$ indicates to which expert observation $(y(t),\mathbf{x}(t))$ belongs.

- distribution of $\textcolor{orange}{Y(t)|Z}$:\
Assuming \textcolor{violet}{$Z=k$}, **FFMoE** considers a concurrent model specific to \textcolor{red}{expert $k$}:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\textcolor{orange}{{y}(t) = \mathbf{X}(t)}\textcolor{red}{\beta_k(t)}+ \textcolor{orange}{\varepsilon(t)}
\end{eqnarray*}
with $\varepsilon(t)\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\textcolor{red}{\sigma_k}^2)$

- distribution of $Z$:\
$p(Z=k)$ (\textcolor{blue}{gated network} function) is assumed to depend on the covariates:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\textcolor{blue}{p(Z=k|\mathbf{X}(t))=\pi_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t))}
\end{eqnarray*}

### The gated network function
- mixing proportions are assumed to depend on the covariates: $$\textcolor{blue}{\pi_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t))}$$
- such assumption is useful for prediction (in order to select which mixture component to use)
- we consider the gating softmax function:
\begin{align*}
    \textcolor{blue}{\pi_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t)) = \frac{\exp(h_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t)))}{1 + \sum_{k'=1}^{{K}-1}\exp(h_{k'}(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_{k'}(t)))}},
\end{align*}
where
\begin{eqnarray*}
 \textcolor{blue}{h_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t)) = \int_0^T\alpha_k^\top(s)\mathbf{X}(s)ds}
\end{eqnarray*}
with $\alpha_k(t) = (\alpha_{k,0}(t), \alpha_{k,1}(t), \ldots, \alpha_{k,p}(t))^\top$.

*Rk: in this model, the mixture proportion is constant over time.*

### Basis expansion assumption
The model is based on the function ${X}^1_i(t),\ldots,{X}^p_i(t)$... **that are not observed**:

- we assume that the functions can be **decomposed into a finite basis of functions** $({B}_j^\ell(t))_{1\leq j\leq q_{{x}^\ell}}$:
\begin{eqnarray*}
    {X}_i^\ell(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{q_{{x}^\ell}}\,x_{ij}^\ell{B}_j^\ell(t) = {B}^\ell(t)^\top x_i^\ell
\end{eqnarray*}
- the same assumption is made for the functional parameters (with not necessary the same basis):
\begin{eqnarray*}
    \beta_{k,\ell}(t) &=& \sum_{j=1}^{q_{\beta^\ell}}\,b_{k,\ell}^{\ell}\,\phi_j^\ell(t) = \phi^\ell(t)^\top b_k^{\ell},\\
    \alpha_{k,\ell}(t) &=& \sum_{j=1}^{{L}_{\alpha^\ell}} a_{k,j}^\ell \varrho_j^\ell(t) = \varrho^\ell(t)^\top a_k^\ell. 
\end{eqnarray*}

### From functional to multivariate model (1/2)
Under the basis expansion assumption:

- the gated network function can be rewritten
\begin{eqnarray*}
 \textcolor{blue}{\pi_k(\mathbf{X}(t),\alpha_k(t))= \frac{\exp(a_k^\top r_i)}{ 1 + \sum_{k'=1}^{{K}-1}\exp(a_{k'}^\top r_i)}:=\pi_k(r_i,a_k)}
\end{eqnarray*}
with $\textcolor{blue}{r_i=\int_{0}^T \varrho(s)^\top {B}(s)ds \ x_i}$
- and the concurrent model of expert $k$
\begin{eqnarray*}
 \textcolor{orange}{{y}_i(t) = {R}_i(t)^\top b_k + \varepsilon_i(t)},
\end{eqnarray*}
where  $\textcolor{orange}{{R}_i(t) = \varphi(t)^\top{B}(t)\,x_i}$.

### From functional to multivariate model (2/2)
- at the sample time points $t_j$, we observe: 
\begin{eqnarray*}
 \textcolor{orange}{{y}_i(t_j) = {R}_i(t_j)^\top b_k + \varepsilon_i(t_j)}
\end{eqnarray*}
- for each $i$, observations $({y}_i(t_j))_j$ **are not independent**
- a **random effect** approach is considered: $\varepsilon_i(t_j)={U}_i + \eta_{ij},$ with $\eta_{ij}$ a Gaussian white noise and ${U}_i$ a random effect variable
- the whole set of observations $\mathbf{Y} =({y}_1(t_1), \dots, {y}_n(t_m))^\top$ can then be modelized by:
\begin{eqnarray*}
 \textcolor{orange}{\mathbf{Y} = {R}^\top b_k + {W} {U}  +  \eta}
\end{eqnarray*}
where
  - $R = (\varphi(t_j)^\top{B}(t_j)\,x_i)_{i,j}$
  - $W$ a block diagonal matrix, where each block is a matrix of 1
  - ${U} = ({U}_1, \ldots, {U}_n)^\top\sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\Gamma)$
  - $\eta = (\eta_{ij})_{i,j}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\sigma^2\mathbb{I}_{nm})$ 
  

### FFMoE likelihood
The log-likelihood of the model is consequently:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\mathcal{L}(\Psi;\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X}) = {\sum_{i=1}^n}\log\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k(r_i,a_k) \Phi_m(\mathbf{y}_i  ; {R}_i^\top b_k,{V}_{k,i})
\end{eqnarray*}

- In such mixture model, this likelihood can not be maximized analytically.
- The EM algorithm should be considered, which is based on the complete likelihood:

\begin{eqnarray*}
\mathcal{L}_c(\Psi;\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X},\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{Z}}) = {\sum_{i=1}^n}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{1}_{Z_i=k}} \log\left(\pi_k(r_i,a_k)) \Phi_m(\mathbf{y}_i  ; {R}_i^\top b_k,{V}_{k,i})\right)
\end{eqnarray*}

### EM for FFMoE likelihood maximization
\begin{eqnarray*}
\mathcal{L}_c(\Psi;\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X},\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{Z}}) = {\sum_{i=1}^n}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{1}_{Z_i=k}} \log\left(\pi_k(r_i,a_k)) \Phi_m(\mathbf{y}_i  ; {R}_i^\top b_k,{V}_{k,i})\right)
\end{eqnarray*}

- init: $\Psi^{(0)}$
- repeat until convergence of $\mathcal{L}$
  - E step:
  compute $$\mathcal{Q}(\Psi,\Psi^{(q)})=E_{\Psi^{(q)}}[\mathcal{L}_c(\Psi;\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X},\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{Z}})|\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X}]$$
  which consists in computing $E_{\Psi^{(q)}}[\textcolor{violet}{\mathbf{1}_{Z_i=k}}|\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X}]$
  - M step:
  compute $$\Psi^{(q+1)}=\text{argmax}_{\Psi}\mathcal{Q}(\Psi,\Psi^{(q)})$$

### Penalized likelihood (PenFFMoE)
To introduce smoothness in the functional coefficients $\beta_{k,\ell}(t)$ and $\alpha_{k,\ell}(t)$, we propose to maximized the **penalized log-likelihood**
\begin{eqnarray*}
    \mathcal{L}_{pen}(\Psi;\mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X}) = \mathcal{L}(\Psi; \mathbf{Y},\mathbf{X}) + {Pen}(\Psi), 
\end{eqnarray*}
where
\begin{eqnarray*}
  {Pen}(\Psi) = {\sum_{k=1}^{K} }\sum_{\ell=0}^p\lambda_{k,\ell}\int\beta^{''}_{k,\ell}(t)^2 dt + {\sum_{k=1}^{{K}-1} } \sum_{\ell=1}^p\gamma_{k,\ell}\int\alpha^{''}_{k,\ell}(t)^2 dt
\end{eqnarray*}
\small
or, in matricial term:
\begin{eqnarray*}
    {Pen}(\Psi) &=& {\sum_{k=1}^{K} }\sum_{\ell=0}^p\lambda_{k,\ell} \sum_{s,u=1}^{{L}_{\beta^\ell}}b_{k,s}^\ell b_{k,u}^\ell\Gamma_{su}^{\ell} + {\sum_{k=1}^{{K}-1} } \sum_{\ell=1}^p\gamma_{k,\ell} \sum_{s,u=1}^{{L}_{\beta^\ell}}a_{k,s}^\ell a_{k,u}^\ell\Upsilon_{su}^{\ell}
\end{eqnarray*}

### Function-on-function regression in R
This model is implemented in the PenFFR package, available at:
https://github.com/JuJacques/Functional-Data

### Results analysis 

BIC selects 4 experts.

We can look to which location belong to each of these mixture expert:

![](images/map_penffmoe.pdf){width=400px}


### Results analysis 

Applying PenFFMoE model works well on Canadian data.

Below are prediction for two randomly chosen stations: 

![](images/pred2_canada.pdf){width=200px}
![](images/pred1_canada.pdf){width=200px}

- Blue points: actual data
- red and green lines: predictions for FFMoE and PenFFMoE
- violet and gold lines:  predictions for PenFFR and pffr.



### Lab 4

Try one of the previous packages to forecast precipitation from temperature.

### Tack-off message

- sequential data are everywhere... and **functional data** is a good way to deal with them

- many works have extended mutlivariate models to functional models (PCA, clustering, classification, regression...)

- many R packages exist (fda, funHDDC, ...), but there is still some lacks, in particular in supervised setting (regression, classification...)
